data.title: September's 5 Dividend Growth Stocks With Yields Up To 6%
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

For some background on this monthly publication, here is my view on dividend growth stocks:

Dividend growth stocks aren't always the most exciting investments out there. They often aren't grabbing the headlines, and they aren't the stocks running up hundreds of percentages in a year. In fact, they are often some of the least exciting stocks. And that is precisely their strongest selling point. With such a vast world of dividend growth stocks available out there, it is important to screen through to see if there are any worthwhile investments to explore.

They are stocks that provide growing wealth over time to income investors. Dividend growers are often larger (not always), more financially stable companies that can pay out reliable cash flows to investors. Some are slower growers than others. Some are going to be cyclical that require a strong economy. Some are going to be secular, which doesn't generally rely on a more robust economy.

Dividend growth can promote share price appreciation. Of course, that is if these companies are growing their earnings to support such dividend growth in the first place. Trust me. There are yield traps out there - I've owned a few that I'm not particularly proud of.

I like to think of investing in dividend stocks as a perpetual loan of sorts. Essentially, every dividend is a repayment of your original capital. Eventually, holding long enough, you have the position "paid off." It is all returned back into your pocket from that point forward.

All of this being said, it is important to understand my approach to dividend stocks and why screening dividend stocks can be important for income investors. As with any initial screening, this is just an initial dive - more due diligence would be necessary before pulling the trigger.

The Parameters For Screening

I'll be using some handy features that Seeking Alpha provides right here on their website for this screen. In particular, I will be screening utilizing their quant grades in dividend safety, dividend growth and dividend consistency.

Dividend Safety is relatively self-explanatory. These will be stocks that SA quants show reasonable safety compared to the rest of their various sectors. The grade considers many different factors, but earnings payout ratios, debt and free cash flow are among these. This category will be stocks with A+ to B- ratings.

For the dividend growth category, we have factors such as the CAGR of various periods relative to other stocks in the same sector. Additionally, the quants also look at earnings, revenue and EBITDA growth. As we will see, this doesn't mean that every stock with a higher grade has the growth we are looking for. This just factors in that the dividend has grown or earnings are growing to support possible dividend growth. For these, the grades will also be A+ through B- ratings.

Finally, for dividend consistency, we want stocks that will be paying reliable dividends for us for a very long time. In particular, hopefully, they are raising yearly, though that isn't an explicit requirement. We will also include stocks with a general uptrend in dividend payments, which means there could have been periods where they paused increases for a year or two.

After looking at those factors alone, we are left with 423 stocks at this time, which is up from last month's 416. I'll link the screen here, though it is a dynamic list that constantly updates regularly. When viewing this article, there could be more or less when going to the link.

From there, I wanted to narrow down the list a lot more. I then sorted the list by TTM dividend yield, from highest to lowest. Since these will be safer dividend stocks in the first place, screening for those among the higher payers shouldn't hurt. Though there are always exceptions, after all, this is simply an initial screening, and I try to note those that I feel aren't as safe as the algorithms are suggesting.

As is usually the case, we have a few repeats that we recently covered, so we'll skip over those until at least a quarter has gone by before providing some quick updates. Those names include RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ), United Parcel Service (UPS), Alpine Income Property Trust (PINE), Hess Midstream (HESM) and Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE).

Another name that made it toward the top of the list this month was Ennis (EBF)—while it might be an interesting investment opportunity, we'll skip over that name as well. It hasn't raised its regular quarterly dividend since 2021.

What that does leave us with is peculiar this month, as it is an interesting list of infrastructure plays, primarily in the energy space. Energy investments such as midstreams and MLPs often pay outsized dividends/distributions, which isn't anything new, but to see all five of the names today being energy infrastructure related hasn't happened before.

#1 HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital (HASI) 6% Yield

This is the first time we've touched on HASI since initially starting this monthly screening piece, going back to December 2021. However, this isn't the first time I've run across HASI, as it is an interesting investment choice in the renewable infrastructure space. With that said, HASI is rather unique in the fact that it operates more like a fund than a traditional operating company. That is, they invest in different projects instead of operating the assets themselves. They are a rather large 'fund' with $14.6 billion in managed assets.

They boast over 1250 investments closed with over 100 client relationships. Of course, as their name would suggest, they are investing in projects such as solar and wind, among other projects. We all know energy demand around the world is only growing, and investing in renewable energy sources is going to be a part of providing that supply.

These investments have translated into predictable earnings and cash flow to pay out to their investors; since 2014, the adjusted EPS has seen a 10% CAGR.

Given the rising earnings, that's translating into a growing dividend for investors. Over the long term, they have a target of around a 50% payout ratio for the dividend, but they are guiding for 2027 to have the payout ratio closer to 55-60%. That means we should expect to see dividend growth slow in the coming years unless earnings surprise to the upside relative to their ~10% long-term estimates.

I believe that HASI is an interesting investment that could be worth exploring even further to see if it would be worthwhile.

#2 Enbridge (ENB) 5.53% Yield

ENB is a name we've come across before, as are the rest of the names we'll be touching on today. It is also one of the more popular energy infrastructure plays as a long-time dividend-growing machine from Canada. However, U.S. investors with a stronger USD haven't quite seen growth in the dividend like their Canadian counterparts due to currency fluctuations.

So Canadians see 30 consecutive years of dividend increases, and Americans would see what looks like flat dividends for the last several years now. For what it is worth, they are continuing to expect 5% growth through the end of the decade.

While the company operates through several different segments, the largest contributor to adjusted EBITDA is their liquids pipelines business. However, gas transmission and gas distribution & storage are also significant enough divisions to move the needle. Their renewable power segment doesn't contribute much, but that is expected to help provide overall earnings growth to continue to support the growing payout to investors.

#3 ONEOK (OKE) 5.52% Yield

OKE is another energy infrastructure play as a midstream company focused primarily on natural gas pipelines and based in the U.S. Their largest segment is natural gas liquids, followed by natural gas gathering and processing, but right behind that is refined products and crude. To a smaller degree, there are natural gas pipelines. That all contributes to 2025's adjusted EBITDA guidance of $8.225 billion.

Adjusted EBITDA growth for this company has been exploding over the last several years. Instead of this being organic growth from the business alone, it has been largely driven by sizeable acquisitions. The latest 2025 estimates are the full year of EnLink and Medallion acquisitions, now being reflected. Assuming no new acquisitions, 2026 estimates are still calling for growth, but that will be a bit more moderate.

As I've been writing covered calls on my TC Energy (TRP) investment, as it looks relatively more expensive than when I bought it several years ago, I've been keeping an eye on this space a bit more, looking for a potential replacement if need be. Interestingly, OKE was one of those names that made the list, as it looks like it has become beaten down.

EV/EBITDA favors OKE currently over TRP, which is one of the reasons it caught my attention as a potential attractively valued alternative. Forward EV/EBITDA is 9.76x for OKE and 13.2x for TRP.

Similar to ENB, OKE boasts a long history of growing its dividend—though it did stall out during the Covid pandemic. That isn't necessarily a negative, in my opinion, as keeping a strong balance sheet is important during times of such uncertainty and potentially being overextended.

As always, this is only an initial screening, and more digging would need to be done to see if OKE would be a decent potential replacement for TRP. However, it is one of my primary considerations at this time.

#4 Pembina Pipeline Corp. (PBA) 5.30% Yield

With PBA, we are back in Canada with another energy transportation and midstream services company. They used to be more unique as they offered a monthly distribution compared to most of their peers, who paid quarterly. However, they transitioned to being a boring quarterly payer again:

Finally, we announced yesterday our intention to move from a monthly to a quarterly common share dividend payment in 2023, payments to be made in March, June, September and December of each year. This change aligns Pembina's dividend practices with the vast majority of its peers and companies within the TSX 60. Subject to approval by the Board of Directors, the monthly dividend is expected to end with the dividend to be declared in early December and paid on December 30. The first quarterly dividend is expected to be effective for the dividend to be paid in March 2023.

One benefit could be keeping cash for longer, earning some interest, but otherwise, I'm not sure why being similar to the vast majority of its peers is a strength. Either way, it is a done deal after decades of them paying out monthly previously. Additionally, similar to ENB, for U.S. investors, due to the CAD/USD currency conversion, it would appear as though they haven't raised their dividend for the last several years.

Instead, we know they've been growing in an upward trend in their annual dividend since 2011. Similar to OKE, they stalled out with consecutive growth in 2020/2021, where they held the annual payout at $2.52.

PBA has made our list previously, but it hasn't been since October 2022. It doesn't necessarily provide the highest yield, but it has been similar to its peers in being able to provide reliable growth over the years. On the valuation front, the EV/EBITDA comes in at 12.15x, and the forward EV/EBITDA comes in at 10.49x. On that basis, it makes it relatively more attractively valued than TRP but not as attractive as OKE.

#5 Canadian Natural Resources Limited (CNQ) 5.20% Yield

Finally, we have CNQ—another Canadian operation, as the name would imply. This isn't the first time this name has shown up, and the last time was just back in March of this year. Since then, shares have seen a small rise, but over the last year, shares have still gone down by over 10%.

Unlike the other names we touched on today, this is going to be a relatively more volatile name, as it isn't a pipeline play but an upstream operation in the gathering and processing space.

That leaves them generally more subject to commodity price fluctuations, whereas pipeline operators typically have high levels of fixed-fee contracts in place to help provide more predictability. CNQ provides an estimate of free cash flow per share based on the price of WTI, and that can start to show us just how impacted the company can be.

However, CNQ is quite diversified in its production mix. That can help to limit some of the negative impacts that one commodity price, such as natural gas, collapses and stays depressed.

It also means that in good times, CNQ can do quite well in terms of its share price appreciating and being able to deliver significant dividend growth.

The growth in the dividend has been significant enough now that even with the currency fluctuations between CAD/USD, it still looks like it is growing for U.S. investors. They paid a significant special dividend in 2022 and, even with being a more volatile business, have managed to have 25 years of consecutive dividend increases.

At a ~21% CAGR since the company's inception, it has been at a significant pace as well. However, the growth has been in more recent years, with the last 5-year CAGR at over 22.5% compared to 15.6% over the last decade. Of course, that is quite impressive nonetheless.

In terms of valuation, it isn't going to be able to be directly compared to the pipeline companies we touched on above. That said, for some context, EV/EBITDA here is 6.05x, with forward EV/EBITDA at 5.98x. This can tell us that investors are seemingly unwilling to pay up for something that is higher risk, such as CNQ; at least that is my impression. However, it also tells us that there isn't too much growth expected going forward since the EV/EBITDA multiple is staying rather flat.

For investors who can handle more risk, CNQ seems to be quite a strong candidate to consider. They've certainly been able to deliver strong and consistent dividend growth.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SCHG: This Is What The Final Stages Of A Bull Market Look Like
data.description:
My previous bearish article on the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHG) didn't age well. In less than three months, the ETF gained another 9.32%. Back then, I stated that SCHG's high concentration in overvalued stocks mirrors past bubbles. I also cautioned readers that we're likely in the latter stages of a long bull market that might lead to disappointing returns going forward.

What's changed since then that justifies revisiting the thesis:

  • Average valuations are even higher than before, rising from a 36x P/E ratio to a 41x.
  • Concentration risk has further increased, as the Magnificent Seven take up an even larger share.
  • Investor sentiment has become more exuberant, as believers slowly start to outweigh the doubters.

Back then, I also didn't mention that the final stage of a long bull market tends to end parabolically. This bull market has been going on for more than 16 years. It could go on for another couple of years, and if history is any guide, the final years tend to be the most profitable for nimble short-term-oriented speculators, as the masses are finally convinced that "this time it's really different".

As a long-term value investor, I stay away from euphoria. If an investment doesn't make sense from a mathematical basis, it's not for me. Well, knowing that I may miss out on a few quick gains.

Just because the markets continue to rise doesn't mean old valuation metrics have become obsolete. The higher the valuations we pay, the more risks we face.

SCHG's average earnings multiple keeps rising, and the expected returns are therefore lower than before. Because of those developments, the argument remains similar—but the urgency and risks are now more pronounced.

We're living in extraordinary times. The S&P 500 and NASDAQ seem unstoppable, and the more the markets rise, the more believers they convert.

The major indices are reaching valuation levels last seen in the late 1990s. It ended in the dot-com crash and the lost decade—a decade of negative nominal returns.

While I don't predict another lost decade (nor can I rule it out), the higher valuations we pay, the higher the risks we face, and the fewer returns we can expect.

Yet, in the short term markets don't behave on a mathematical and statistical basis. The longer a bull market rages on, the more investors start believing that it'll go on forever. The average investor becomes bullish at exactly the wrong time.

I clearly remember the sentiment ten years ago. Back then, there was a widespread fear that markets were in a bubble. I got asked if it's time to sell out.

My response was simple: "Markets usually don't crash when everyone is expecting it. Only when most investors become complacent is it time to worry".

Ten years later, and valuations are in the stratosphere. It seems like fewer investors worry about valuations, even though they have become more worrisome.

Long bull markets typically end in euphoria. In other words, the final stages are often the most fun. People quit their day jobs to participate in day trading. Endless prosperity is not only a possibility but a certainty.

Given the current sentiment, the major indices might still have room to run, long bull markets usually end parabolically. But it could also end tomorrow, and that's the risk.

The best advice I can give to long-term investors is to dampen your expectations for the next decade, and most importantly, don't sell out when optimism turns into widespread fear.

With that in mind, let's put the doom and gloom briefly aside and dive deeper into the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG).

Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG)

SCHG offers access to large-cap U.S. equities that exhibit growth style characteristics, according to Schwab.

The net expense ratio is 0.04% and the turnover rate is 3.00%. Both are low; therefore, SCHG is a low-cost ETF, which I view favorably as a long-term-oriented investor. But there are "hidden" fees—namely, the valuations you're paying for the underlying holdings at current prices.

P/E Ratio

Yahoo Finance claims the P/E ratio is 35.91, while the Wall Street Journal has it at 40.89. Different providers calculate the P/E ratio differently, but I usually rely on the WSJ numbers, as they tend to reflect the earnings multiple on a TTM basis quite accurately.

A 41x earnings multiple is steep. The last time the NASDAQ was trading at similar valuations was in 1998. Back then, there were some warning voices, but over the short term, they were wrong.

From 1998 to early 2000, the NASDAQ gained more than 300%, only to decline by 80% in the two years thereafter.

Long-term investors, who bought growth stocks in 1998, had to wait for more than a decade to achieve positive nominal total returns. That's the risk we're facing by paying more than 40x earnings, but over the short term, it could go either way. Over the medium term, there's a heightened risk of dismal total returns.

The biggest risk is selling out at exactly the wrong time, a common error made by the majority of investors.

"Everybody has a plan until they get punched in the face" - Mike Tyson

We plan to invest for the long term, but if challenges arise, the long term can become the short term. By mentally preparing for the likely challenges ahead, we could belong to the small group of successful investors who see opportunity in a bear market at a time when others are panicking. Never waste a good crisis.

Dividend Yield

Growth investors usually don't care about dividends as long as prices keep rising. It's only when greed turns into fear that dividends become important again. Right now, SCHG is yielding 0.43%, which can be described in one word: negligible.

We're in the Latter Stages of a Bull Market

As investors, we bet on probabilities, not on certainties. We're living in an uncertain world with an uncertain future. That's not a popular thing to say as an analyst, but it's the truth. And the truth will set you free.

All we can do is to estimate the odds and bet when the odds are in our favor. Right now, the odds don't seem to be in our favor when it comes to ETFs like SCHG. The markets are showing signs of excess. Worthless altcoins with very little, if any, use value are trading at more than a trillion-dollar market cap.

A seemingly declining car company with high fixed costs is demanding a triple-digit P/FCF ratio. An AI software company is trading at 250x P/FCF. The NASDAQ and S&P 500 are trading at valuation levels only seen once in over a century, the ones in the late 1990s.

Those are not the characteristics of a cautious environment but the latter stages of a bull market.

This bull market started in March 2009. It has been going on for 16 years. Even if we're in the latter stages, it could go on for another 1 or 2 years. Maybe even longer if we were to reach Nikkei 1989 levels, which peaked around 60 to 70 times earnings. If the S&P 500 were to rise to those levels, we'd witness another 100% gain.

But the higher this bull market peaks, the more painful the following bear market will likely be. And as long as the world is still cyclical and recessions and economic turmoil aren't a thing of the past, there will be downturns.

It took the Nikkei roughly 30 years to bottom after the excesses in 1989. The U.S. indices fared better after the dot-com bust, but a decade of negative nominal returns is still painful. Again, most investors sold out and made temporary losses permanent.

The biggest risk over the coming decade isn't tariffs or geopolitics, it's overpaying and multiple compression.

Similarities to the Nifty Fifty Bubble

As mentioned before, as investors, we're betting on probabilities, not certainties. I could be wrong.

The Magnificent Seven have shown annual earnings growth of 20%+ over the last few years. If this growth continued, the valuations right now could be considered reasonable.

Given the size of the companies, the large investments in AI without a clear path to decent ROIC over the medium term, as well as the fact that we're living in a cyclical world, I view endless growth as a small probability, but nonetheless, it is a probability.

It's not the first time that investors believed in endless prosperity. Back in the early 1970s, there was a group of roughly 50 large-cap stocks called the Nifty Fifty. They were considered "one-decision stocks", you bought them and never sold.

Just like the Magnificent Seven, they were growing at 20%+ annually, and investors expected them to grow endlessly. "Trees don't grow to the sky" is a famous saying, but the more prices rise, the more investors seem to forget.

At the height of the bubble, some Nifty Fifty stocks were trading at triple-digit P/E ratios, but the average stock was trading at 42x earnings.

Then the 1973-1974 recession came, growth slowed down, and investors started to panic. A small change can lead to staggering losses when we're paying high multiples.

Many of the Nifty Fifty stocks declined by 70%, 80%, 90% or more. In less than two years, the average P/E ratio fell into the single digits.

That would have been the perfect time to load up on the Procter & Gambles, the Coca-Colas, and the Johnson & Johnsons of this world, but only a few brave souls (including Warren Buffett) took advantage of it. Most investors bought into the hype and sold out into the fear. If you only extract one lesson from this article, don't let that be you!

Sector Allocation

The short history lesson was necessary to understand what kind of environment we're in. With that in mind, let's continue on SCHG.

Not surprisingly, as a growth ETF, SCHG is heavily exposed to tech. It's the largest sector, with 48.61% of total assets invested. Tech companies can also be found under communication or consumer cyclical, so the allocation could be higher.

Top 10 Holdings

SCHG holds 230 stocks, and the top 10 holdings represent 57.28% of total assets. It's basically a concentrated bet on just a few names trading at elevated multiples. What could go wrong?

NVIDIA leads the list with 11.23% of assets invested. It's followed by Microsoft, Apple, Amazon, and Broadcom.

A Few Words on the Top Holdings

If you're paying an average of 42x earnings, there better be decent growth ahead; otherwise, you're vastly overpaying. NVIDIA is the largest holding, which, to me, is worrying. To quote from one of my previous articles:

AI is still in its infancy, let's call it the gold rush phase. So far, NVIDIA holds a strong position in supplying the shovels to the miners seeking their fortune, but the basics of our capitalistic system shouldn't be forgotten. Whenever there are outsized returns, competition is at your back, and I wouldn't bet on NVIDIA holding the same position years from now.

Also, the AI boom could slow down substantially during the next recession, or if the players find out that it isn't as profitable as they once hoped it would be.

Whenever there are revolutionary technologies showing up, like the internet, railroads, canals, and now AI, there's usually an initial investing boom where the crowd gets excited. This is usually followed by a huge correction, like we saw during 2000-2003 after the dot-com exuberance.

If you're really a believer in AI, the next large crash will be the opportunity to load up on your favorite shares. But having experienced the markets long enough, whenever prices are low, there just aren't that many believers around.

As a long-term-oriented investor, I find it highly unlikely that NVIDIA can keep its high margins and growth rate over the long term. Yet, when you're paying a 50x P/E ratio, that's already priced in. If the growth doesn't materialize, look out below.

I also find it unlikely that companies like Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta will be able to sustain their growth rates over the long term. Size matters for growth—ask Berkshire Hathaway. The next recession will likely be painful for stocks trading above 40x earnings multiples. A lot of the (potential) growth has already been priced in.

Tesla's car sales have been declining rapidly, and the brand seems permanently damaged, yet the stock price doesn't seem to care and is trading at over 200x earnings. A clear sign of exuberance and excess in the markets. Over the short term, narratives win. Over the long term, fundamentals win.

Comparison Against Other ETFs

Let's compare SCHG's total returns to its peers. The best advice I can give to investors is to stop extrapolating the past into the future. "Past performance is not indicative of future returns"—what if it's true?

Since the beginning of the bull market in 2009, growth stocks have performed exceptionally well. SCHG leads the list on a total return basis, which makes it a popular choice for growth investors.

The great performance of the past has led to elevated valuations in the present, which could lead to disappointing returns in the future.

ETFs like SCHG will likely be much higher than now decades from now, but in between, there will be painful bear markets. The higher the valuations you pay, the more likely you'll get caught in one.

At current valuations, I can't help but rate it a "sell". This rating doesn't mean long-term investors should sell out. Missing the reentry point and potential capital gains taxes are all important factors to consider.

The higher the capital gains taxes, the less it makes sense for long-term investors to sell down, even though valuations appear risky at this point. When it comes to entering new positions, I'd wait for better entry points at lower valuations.

This article is just a little reminder that it might be time to dampen our expectations for the coming decade, and most importantly, prepare mentally so we don't sell out due to misleading expectations that weren't met.

Conclusion

Investing is less about predicting the next move and more about positioning to thrive across market cycles. There's no doubt that SCHG holds some of the best companies in the world, but even great businesses can be poor investments at the wrong price.

The current valuations are mirroring the valuation levels in the late 1990s and during the Nifty Fifty bubble in the early 1970s. While history doesn't repeat, it often rhymes.

If you're a long-term investor, that doesn't mean panic. It means preparing mentally for a market that may disappoint over the next decade. In the short term, it could go either way, but there's a heightened chance for dismal returns over the medium term. It might be wise to preserve capital to deploy it whenever better opportunities arise.

data.title: Markets Still Expect Lower Yields After Fed Rate Cut
data.description:
Thank you, sir, may I have another?

That’s the main takeaway for markets-based rate cut expectations following the Federal Reserve’s widely expected ¼-point cut for its target rate announced on Wednesday. Although key Treasury yields popped yesterday (suggesting anxiety about rate cuts when inflation is edging higher and is well above the Fed’s 2% target), market signals still point to expectations that the Fed will continue to ease monetary policy. The driving factor for the Fed’s decision: a slowing labor market, which the central bank sees as the bigger risk at the moment vs. inflation.

Explaining the cut, the Fed’s FOMC statement advised: “Recent indicators suggest that growth of economic activity moderated in the first half of the year. Job gains have slowed, and the unemployment rate has edged up but remains low. Inflation has moved up and remains somewhat elevated.”

The central bank’s revised rate outlook, released yesterday as part of new economic projections, anticipates the median Fed funds rate (at 4.125% after yesterday’s cut) will continue sliding. The ”projected appropriate policy path” for the target rate for 2025 is 3.6%, slipping further to 3.4% for 2026 - moderately below the current 4.125% median.

Fed funds futures are pricing in another ¼-point cut at the next policy meeting in Oct, and a repeat performance is expected for the December meeting. By the end of the year, futures are pricing in high odds that the target range will drop to a 3.50%-to-3.75% range, in line with the Fed’s current outlook.

The Treasury market also sees more rate cuts brewing, based on the policy-sensitive 2-year Treasury yield. Although this widely followed rate rose sharply yesterday to 3.57%, it remains well below the current median Fed funds rate - a gap that suggests the market is still expecting rate cuts.

A simple model that compares the current median Fed funds rate to the sum of the unemployment rate and the annual pace of headline consumer inflation (a proxy for monitoring conditions re: the Fed’s dual mandate) suggests that policy is still modestly tight. In turn, that leaves room for another rate cut to align policy with a neutral level vs. current economic conditions.

A possible joker in the deck is the risk that inflation will continue to edge up - a risk that Fed Chairman Powell noted in his press conference yesterday:

We have begun to see goods prices showing through into higher inflation, and actually the increase in goods prices accounts for most of the increase in inflation, or perhaps all of the increase in inflation over the course of this year. Those are not very large effects at this point, and we do expect them to continue to build over the course of the rest of the year and into next year.

For now, the Fed sees the slowdown in hiring as a bigger threat. “It’s really the risks that we’re seeing to the labor market that were the focus of today’s decision,” Powell explained.

But as I discussed yesterday at TMC Research, the rise in core consumer inflation in recent months, based on the annualized 3-month change, could be a warning sign that pricing pressure will accelerate further. If so, expectations for more rate cuts may be premature.

For the moment, markets are still pricing in more rate cuts. The question is whether incoming inflation numbers will persuade the crowd to reprice expectations?

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Goldman Sachs Emerging Markets Equity Insights Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

In the second quarter of 2025, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index rose by 11.99% amid significant volatility caused by tariff-related announcements on Liberation Day. Asian Emerging Markets were particularly affected but later recovered, slightly outpacing developed markets. Domestic investors seized this as a buying opportunity, increasing their exposure, while foreign institutional investors (FIIs) bought around $30 billion from April lows, reversing about one-third of their previous year's selling¹ As mentioned, East Asia saw a strong recovery, with Taiwan and Korea both rising over 30%, outperforming the broader EM index by 15-20%.¹ Geopolitical tensions, especially between India and Pakistan and in the Middle East, had limited impact on this recovery.

Performance Review

In Q2 2025, the Goldman Sachs Emerging Markets Equity Insights Fund returned 11.61%, underperforming the MSCI Emerging Markets Index by 38bps (net, I-share).

Our Themes and Trends investment pillar was the largest contributor to excess returns across the quarter. Notably, our overweight positioning to Korean Aerospace & Defense stocks proved additive to excess returns, as these names benefitted from the broad trend of European rearmament amid fears of a US defense pullback.

The Sentiment Analysis investment pillar was relatively flat across the period, with positioning contributory to excess returns in the same Korean Industrials names offsetting losses from our overweight position in Chinese Consumer Discretionary names, which struggled in the quarter amid weak domestic consumer demand.

Our High-Quality Business Models pillar was also quite flat across the quarter, with our overweight towards Taiwanese Semiconductor names, which performed strongly amid surging global demand for AI and high-performance computing chips, helping offset other losses.

The Fundamental Mispricings investment pillar had the most challenging quarter. This was in large part due to positioning in Indian Information Technology names which were challenged due to their revenue exposure to the United States – especially following the Trump administration’s Liberation Day announcements.

Country Tilts proved additive to performance in the period due to an overweight towards Mexico, whose equity markets benefitted from constructive Central Bank policies and a more muted impact from US tariffs than expected.

The Fund’s investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted above. Please visit am.gs.com to obtain the most recent month-end returns. data.title: Quarterly Vs. Semiannual Reporting: Revisiting Trump's Controversial Proposal
data.description:
Introduction

When I first saw President Trump’s suggestion to shift U.S. companies from quarterly to semiannual reporting, my immediate reaction was negative, and the first thought I had was, Why would I want less transparency and more risk as a result? It was the first time I had thought about this topic and had an opinion on it, as back in 2018, when it was proposed for the first time, I wasn’t even following the news and wasn’t yet investing.

But after reading academic research, expert commentary, and policy debates, my view became more nuanced. I still believe quarterly reporting is the backbone of market transparency and something that ultimately protects investors. Moreover, today’s progress is so fast, most big companies would have nothing to talk about if the reporting were every month. But at the same time, I now understand why some regulators and executives argue that semiannual reporting could ease burdens for some firms and reduce the relentless obsession with short-term numbers. Thankfully, we have a huge foreign experience to see all the pluses and minuses.

This article is my attempt to bring those perspectives together, grounded in research but also in my own experience as an investor and economics student. I’m not an academic professor or researcher myself, at least not yet, but I believe I’ve gathered enough interesting facts and opinions to shed some light on this topic.

The Appeal of Semiannual Reporting

There are two main arguments from President Trump for reducing reporting frequency. The first one is the costs. Trump says it burdens the companies and makes them pay a lot of money to get these done, and he is right. It’s not cheap. This is a full-time job for a whole department of people inside the company, and there are always external auditors who are being paid as well. Preparing 10-Q filings takes enormous time and money, especially for small-cap or newly public companies.

As Matthew Abentate explained it (Forbes' former contributor), quarterly reporting can feel like an unfair burden when you’re a startup with three employees sharing an office, but you’re required to meet the same obligations as a multinational with billions in revenue. I haven’t thought about that before, but now that I have, I support the fact that smaller companies shouldn’t be forced into as heavy reporting as the large companies. They have little going on. At the same time, I believe it should be a choice that each small-cap company makes.

The second major argument is the time managers spend addressing those reports. Besides the report itself, there’s also the constant cycle of conference calls, guidance updates, and media appearances they need (or more accurately, often want) to make.

There are many discussions around the appeal of cancelling guidance and earnings calls altogether. Here, I disagree, because numbers don’t always tell a full story. Often, we can understand how well or badly things are by the tone of the call, by some phrases or explanations. You may call it speculation, but it’s really the choice of each retail investor whether to listen to those. I, personally, always read the latest transcript before breaking down the company, and for me, it’s important. Even on the level of personal feelings about the management. I always listen to myself when making decisions, and sometimes, something doesn’t feel right with me, even though the numbers look well. In situations like that, I won’t start a position.

For smaller firms operating in less visible segments of the market, quarterly earnings calls are often the only way to attract investor attention and establish credibility. I am not sure if you ever thought about this, but public companies compete not only with their industry peers but also with all the other public companies for investors' attention. In contrast, we could say that for big firms that already have extensive analyst coverage and everyone is talking about them, the informational value of such events is relatively limited, but the preparation and execution are often long and expensive. So in theory, reducing the frequency of mandatory calls has the potential to ease the burden and allow management to redirect time and effort toward other, more important tasks for business purposes.

A Brown University study brought the same arguments to its conclusion. The authors said that a bifurcated system (allowing smaller companies to report semiannually while keeping quarterly reporting for larger firms) could bring the right balance between long-term value creation and investor protection:

We would suggest that a mid-ground might make sense: adopting a bifurcated reporting system appropriately addresses the major issues with quarterly reporting for small-cap companies, but holds mid and large-cap companies to the current reporting regimen. Under such a system, smaller-sized companies would be allowed to report on a semi-annual basis, while mid and larger businesses would continue to disclose quarterly. The higher cost of compliance for smaller companies compared to larger ones (as a percentage of revenue) leads to an asymmetric impact on capital expenditures and reinvestment. Quarterly reporting is a negligible cost for large-cap companies, but has been shown to adversely impact smaller businesses from both a monetary and time perspective. Reduced reporting requirements would allow small-cap company executives to spend less time on quarterly filings and more on projects that maximize long-term shareholder value. In addition, cost savings from semi-annual reporting can be allocated towards projects that unlock long-term shareholder value.

I have a hard time with this argument. As we will see further in this article, according to the example from other countries who already adopted the system, large caps are those that choose to continue reporting quarterly, even if there is a choice. Smaller caps should be able to report less frequently, but then, they will be less visible, and as a result, the interest from investors could be smaller. I am afraid they will cut costs, but at the same time, these stocks will always be cheaper compared to the more visible peers. So is this a win? I am not sure.

Now, don’t get me wrong, I can see the problem. It’s loud and clear. Reporting and accounting are too complex. Anyone who’s ever taken an accounting course knows how many loopholes and adjustments exist in financial statements. Maybe, if the system of reporting weren’t so difficult, half of the problem would disappear? Maybe it’s not the frequency but the level of difficulty we should be discussing. Companies take up to a month and a half to make those reports, and they finish in the middle of the next quarter. Maybe if the reports were more straightforward, with less room for loopholes and different ways of presenting information, the burden would be smaller. Instead of fighting over the calendar, I’d argue for a committee that rethinks the entire system, aiming for more simplicity and clarity. But it’s another huge topic for the discussion.

The Appeal of Quarterly Reporting

The case against semiannual reporting is just as strong, if not stronger. One of the central arguments made by proponents of less frequent disclosure is that quarterly reporting pressures managers into prioritizing short-term results at the expense of long-term investment. The logic is that the constant spotlight discourages firms from undertaking projects with longer payback periods and instead prioritizes short-term gains.

Yet a Columbia study on the U.K. experience challenges this assumption. When the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) brought in quarterly reporting in 2007 in the U.K., firms did not reduce their long-term investment activity. The expected decline in capital expenditures, R&D, or other long-term projects never actually occurred. Later, when the FCA pulled back the mandatory quarterly reporting in 2014, the opposite did not happen either: firms that switched back to semiannual reporting did not suddenly increase their investment levels. So, it seems like there is no correlation between reporting quarterly or semiannually on the long-term management decisions, at least in the U.K.

What did change, however, was their visibility. Those that stopped making quarterly reports lost analyst coverage and market attention, proving my previous point.

The Bremen study on EU deregulation came to a similar conclusion. Even when firms switched back to semiannual reporting, the supposed benefits of reducing managerial myopia didn’t materialize. An explanation of "Managerial myopia" from Google:

Managerial myopia is a short-sighted decision-making bias where managers prioritize immediate, short-term financial gains and benefits over long-term strategic development and sustainability.

This study shows that short-termism persisted. Capital market effects like liquidity worsened temporarily, but the real pressures on managers didn’t go away. Besides, quarterly reporting, according to the Brown study, lowers the cost of equity by giving investors more frequent, reliable information. Less frequent reporting could push investors to rely on rumors and peer comparisons more heavily, and it won’t work in the best interest of the company and my personal interest as a retail investor.

Managerial Myopia

This is a whole separate topic, I believe that deserves to be raised here. Research from Harvard Business School makes an important point that the real issue isn’t quarterly reporting itself, but managerial incentives. Public companies are far more likely than private ones to sacrifice long-term opportunities for short-term gains, largely because management compensation is directly tied to stock prices. And it’s great from one perspective, because otherwise, businesses would have never acted in investors' interests, and it would often create huge internal problems.

I could talk about this a lot, bringing dozens of different arguments on the organizational behaviors, but I will keep it short. The goals of each team/department/person/manager in an organization, especially a profitable one, are different. Most often, a person follows their own interests and not the interests of the business. Managerial incentives, in many senses, make sure the business grows and develops. This system is not perfect, but it works so far.

Besides, my own argument for managerial myopia is in human psychology. I judge by myself mostly, but bear with me for a minute and see if there is something in those thoughts. As a student, an investor, and someone who shares my work publicly, I feel enormous pressure to always “show results.” When my progress is steady for weeks without a big jump, I start doubting myself. It’s human nature nowadays, especially among the hard-working population. We crave visible progress and fast wins.

Taking it to the corporate world, this pressure is amplified. Have you ever been on Wall Street or in the financial area of Manhattan? Executives, fund managers, and analysts live in a small ecosystem where reputation is currency. They all talk and follow each other on LinkedIn, in those earnings calls, media appearances, etc. All of it fuels the race. The same goes for other public companies and their managers who compete with one another. In that environment, stopping to take a long-term view is hard. So I am not sure if quarterly reporting really creates this cycle; I think it just makes it more visible.

Conclusion

When I first started digging into this topic, I thought quarterly reporting was necessary for transparency and the only real way to keep companies accountable. And to some extent, I still believe that. The evidence from the U.K. and EU shows that cutting back to semiannual reporting doesn’t magically encourage more long-term investment, but it reduces analyst coverage and visibility, making smaller companies even less competitive in the fight for investor attention. Besides, for me, as a retail investor, that lack of transparency is a risk I wouldn’t want to take. I am not a trader, but we live in a fast world, and keeping track of what’s going on with my money once every 6 months feels unreliable.

At the same time, I’ve come to appreciate the burden quarterly reporting brings, especially on small firms. For a startup or small-cap company, the cost and distraction of constant filings are huge. A bifurcated system, like the one suggested in the Brown University study, might be a reasonable compromise, although it’s not a perfect solution either. Managerial myopia most likely is not created by quarterly reporting.

So, this is where I stand: I would suggest not looking for quick fixes for complex problems. The reporting is a problem, and it’s great that it’s being addressed. The whole system invites opportunistic behaviors among some companies, which was supported by the studies I addressed in this article. Meaning, companies use loopholes to their advantage, and semiannual reporting can worsen the issue. Maybe this is the main issue. Creating a clearer and more transparent reporting system would be the real fix that businesses and investors could use.

So I believe quarterly reporting should stay, especially for large companies, because it protects investors and strengthens the market. But at the same time, small companies should be able to be more flexible. Even more importantly, it would be nice to work on simplifying the reporting process itself.

data.title: Goldman Sachs International Equity Insights Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The MSCI EAFE Index rose by 11.78% in the second quarter of 2025. Japanese equities experienced significant volatility, largely driven by continued tariff negotiations. Despite these headwinds, Japanese companies saw a surge in share buybacks, and foreign investors remained net buyers. In Europe, equity performance throughout the quarter was shaped by ongoing uncertainty surrounding US economic and geopolitical policies. The imposition of tariffs on automobiles contributed to market losses, with additional pressure felt across industries such as luxury goods, consumer discretionary, and pharmaceuticals.

Nevertheless, the broader Information Technology sector benefited from AI-related momentum, providing some support to the market. Persistent geopolitical tensions, including the Russia-Ukraine conflict and unrest in the Middle East, continued to drive up energy prices.

Performance Review

In Q2 2025, our International Equity Insights Fund returned 13.33%, outperforming its MSCI EAFE Index benchmark by 155bps over the period (net, I-share). All four of our investment pillars contributed to excess returns over the period.

The Sentiment Analysis investment pillar was the strongest performer in the second quarter, with our overweight positioning towards European Industrial names proving particularly additive to excess returns in the period. Such names performed strongly in the quarter amid strong Eurozone demand and AI/datacenter related tailwinds.

Our Fundamental Mispricings pillar was also additive to performance in the period. Our suite of signals focused on relative industry valuations guided an underweight positioning towards certain French consumer discretionary names, which struggled across the quarter due to tariff-related uncertainties, consumer conservatism, and an underwhelming earnings season.

Our suite of signals seeking to define and monitor cross-stock economic linkages, operating under our Themes and Trends investment pillar, guided an underweight positioning towards European healthcare names that was additive to excess returns. These names struggled amid the same themes of US trade-policy uncertainty that dragged other sectors.

Finally, our High-Quality Business Models pillar also contributed to performance, although to a smaller extent. Losses stemming from our underweight positioning towards Spanish Financials were offset by contributory positioning to the same Consumer Discretionary and Health Care names within Europe.

Country Tilts also contributed to performance across the period, with our overweight to France proving especially additive across the period.

data.title: Cracker Barrel Changes Its Plans
data.description:
I’ve been following the news about Cracker Barrel (NASDAQ:CBRL), but wanted to wait for the 2025 fourth-quarter report to come out before writing an article about it. This report is a good example of why a company’s guidance is more important than the current quarter’s results. Cracker Barrel reported 5.4 percent same-store sales growth, which sounds good by itself. But this was because the restaurant raised its menu prices, not because more customers showed up. And now the restaurant expects to have fewer customers next year as well.

Many people made social media posts about boycotting Cracker Barrel after it changed its logo and it looks like they followed through on their plans. During the fourth-quarter earnings call, CFO Craig Pommells reported that traffic declined 8 percent after the logo change and is expected to be down 7 to 8 percent for the first quarter of 2026 as well. The restaurant is now projecting that traffic will be down 4 to 7 percent for the full year of 2026.

Cracker Barrel also reported $3.48 billion in revenue for 2025 and projects revenue of $3.35 to $3.45 billion for 2026. Its revenue for 2024 was $3.47 billion. So it looks like Cracker Barrel’s long-term revenue growth is flat or slightly negative and this is only because it’s been increasing menu prices. It now expects adjusted EBITDA of $150-$190 million next year as well. If you take the midpoint of $170 million, that’s a 24 percent drop from $224 million in 2025.

After the report was released after hours on September 17, 2025, Cracker Barrel’s stock price fell 9.3 percent to $45. Obviously, the news doesn’t look good for this restaurant right now. But value investors might still be interested in the potential of its long-term turnaround plan. If you’re not aware of this turnaround plan and how that’s been going, I provided a short summary in the next section of this article.

Cracker Barrel’s Turnaround Plan

In 2023, Julie Masino became the CEO of Cracker Barrel and created a multi-step turnaround plan that would be carried out over the next few years. Previous quarters’ reports provided evidence that this turnaround plan was working. In its report for the third quarter of 2025, the restaurant said that same-store sales had risen for the fourth quarter in a row and EPS beat expectations as well. But soon after that, Cracker Barrel rolled out a new logo without the “old timer” Uncle Herschel.

The controversy over the new logo brought more attention to the other changes Cracker Barrel was making, such as remodeling the interiors of its restaurants and simplifying its food preparation processes. The New York Post talked to Cracker Barrel’s employees about some of these food preparation changes. Many of the restaurant’s customers didn’t like these changes and complained about them online. Even President Trump shared his opinion on the restaurant’s logo change. Now Cracker Barrel has brought back the old logo and canceled its store remodeling plan.

Cracker Barrel was originally planning to spend $700 million on remodeling its restaurants, and critics also complained about that because they wanted the restaurant to focus on improving its food. Now the restaurant has freed up more cash for other purposes. Cracker Barrel still plans to spend $135 to $150 million to fix up its restaurants next year, but this budget will now mostly be spent on maintenance activities such as replacing light bulbs and ensuring that restrooms and parking lots are in good condition.

It also appears that Cracker Barrel is going to spend the money that it saved by canceling its remodeling project on stock buybacks instead. The restaurant has now budgeted $100 million for stock buybacks. This buyback plan is also an upside risk factor for investors who think that Cracker Barrel looks like a good stock to short right now. Any 2026 stock price forecast for Cracker Barrel should consider the effect of stock buybacks.

Comparative Valuation

Cracker Barrel has a 2026 projected EBITDA of $170 million and 2026 projected revenue of $3.40 billion if you take the midpoint of both of its forecasts. At a price of $45 per share with 22.4 million shares outstanding, Cracker Barrel has a market cap of $1.01 billion. If you add $1.2 billion in debt and $10 million in cash, that’s an enterprise value of $2.22 billion.

So you can calculate that Cracker Barrel has a forward EV/EBITDA of $2.22 billion / $170 million, or 13.1, and a forward EV/sales of $2.22 billion / $3.40 billion, or 0.65. According to Seeking Alpha, the average forward EV/EBITDA ratio for a restaurant stock was 11.79 and the average forward EV/sales ratio was 1.34. So Cracker Barrel has a higher EV/EBITDA ratio than its peers but trades at less than half of the average EV/sales multiple.

If you argue that Cracker Barrel is undervalued because of its low EV/sales ratio, you’d have to argue that it could increase its profit margin significantly. This would require either increasing menu prices further or making more efficiency improvements. During the earnings call, management explained that Cracker Barrel’s average check is $15 and this is lower than the average family-dining restaurant at $18.

So Cracker Barrel is still more affordable than other restaurants and may still have some room to raise prices. And the restaurant is still improving its efficiency by simplifying the food prep process as well. But it expects traffic to fall significantly in 2026, so it probably doesn’t have a lot of pricing power right now. It’s likely that Cracker Barrel considered these factors when it made its 2026 revenue and EBITDA projections, though. And again, if you take the midpoint of its 2026 EBITDA projection, the restaurant expects a 24 percent drop.

Calculating a One-Year Price Target for Cracker Barrel

After its earnings came out on September 17, Cracker Barrel had a stock price of $45 and 22.4 million shares outstanding. If it spends $100 million on stock buybacks next year, it would reduce its share count by 2.2 million, so it would have 20.2 million shares outstanding. That’s an 11 percent reduction in share count.

If you use the median forward EV/EBITDA for the restaurant industry of 11.79 as a guideline, Cracker Barrel would have an enterprise value of $170 million times 11.79, which is $2.00 billion. If you subtract $1.2 billion in debt and $10 million in cash, that’s a market cap of $790 million. Divide the market cap by 20.2 million shares outstanding, and that’s a one-year price projection of $39 per share. That’s 13 percent lower than the current price of $45 per share.

It’s also risky for the restaurant to maintain a cash balance of $10 million when it has a debt load of $1.2 billion, but it can cancel or delay its stock buyback plan if it needs more liquidity. And it can also cut its dividend if it needs to do that. Of course, either decision would cause its stock price to fall further, but I don’t think Cracker Barrel is at risk of running out of money.

Conclusion

Cracker Barrel’s 2026 forecast projects that it will attract fewer diners next year and it will also earn less income. It’s also canceled one of the main elements of its turnaround strategy, its restaurant remodeling plan, because customers didn’t like it. While it’s using some of the money it saved from canceling its plans on stock buybacks, this won’t be enough to make up for the decline in performance that it now expects next year. So I’d say that Cracker Barrel is a sell.

data.title: Goldman Sachs High Yield Floating Rate Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The US Leveraged Loan Market returned +2.33% over the second quarter of 2025, as elevated policy rates/current yield, favorable technicals (i.e. strong CLO demand & low net issuance), and a recovery in macro sentiment drove performance.

Leveraged credit continued to outperform investment grade peers in the second quarter, although equities significantly outperformed amidst the return to “risk on” following April 9th tariff pauses. Macroeconomic uncertainty faded during the period, with positive data prints coupled with tariff pauses opening the door to smaller-than-expected economic policy impact. On spreads, the loan market discount margin to three years tightened by 39 bps over the quarter to 459 bps, moving 16 bps inside the levels seen at 2024 year end.

Amongst the ratings cohorts, lower quality relatively outperformed. Specifically, CCC/Split CCC-rated loans returned +2.64%, while the B-rated and CCC-rated cohorts returned +2.45% and +2.15%, respectively. All twenty sectors in the loan market were positive over the quarter, with key gainers including Food & Drug (+3.81%), Housing (+3.51%) and Consumer Durables (+3.12%) while relative laggards included Utility (+1.40%), Metals/Minerals (+1.79%) and Food/Tobacco (+1.80%).

On the supply side, new issue activity tapered off into April on tariff fears before picking up slowly to close out the quarter. The second quarter finished with total gross issuance of $103.9bn as compared to first quarter figures of $336.8bn. Consistent with the first quarter, M&A related issuance continued to grow, rising to ~30% of gross issuance, with total net new paper of roughly $42bn beating out first quarter figures despite the lower headline total (source: JP Morgan). On the demand front, healthy CLO issuance continues to drive the major demand for loans with quarterly net CLO issuance totaling +$50.3bn. Fund flows flipped starkly negative this quarter, driven by record outflows in April (-$11.1bn) with a total of $8.2bn, leaving the asset class.

Lastly, the last 12-month par-weighted loan default rate, including distressed exchanges, ended the quarter 9bps lower q/q at 3.79% (source: JP Morgan). The figure ex-distressed exchanges is just 1.36%.

Please refer to the Fund’s prospectus for the most recent expenses

Performance Attribution

The Fund outperformed its benchmark on a net basis over the quarter. Our overweight to market beta (risk), tactical CDX exposure, and holdings of high yield bonds contributed to performance. On the other hand, our cash holdings, tactical allocation to EUR-denominated loans, and our holdings of equity received through restructuring/refinancings hurt performance.

Industry: Our overweights to Building Materials and Energy, as well as our underweight to Food, Beverage, & Tobacco aided performance. Meanwhile, our overweight to Basic Industry as well as our underweights to Technology and Non-Cable Media detracted from returns.

Market Outlook

Overview: We remain constructive on the leveraged loan market while acknowledging tariff uncertainty has clouded the macroeconomic growth outlook to some degree. Issuer credit metrics, on average, remain solid relative to the past 15 years and would benefit from lower policy rates, should they move lower. While lower policy rates would trim income return, we note that the decline in loan yields will likely lag policy rate cuts by several months and today’s yields are attractive relative to other fixed income sectors. Lastly, strong CLO issuance continues to create solid demand for the asset class.

Credit Quality & Curve: We remain selective and favor higher quality loans where higher interest costs have a relatively lower impact on issuer free cash flows. Further, we are looking for select opportunities in the lower rated cohort where fundamentally good businesses would benefit from a decrease in policy rates and create the potential for rating upgrades.

Issuer Type: We are overweight capital structures comprised of both loans and high yield bonds versus loan-only situations where recoveries have been weaker, historically.

Loan Type: We remain underweight smaller first lien loan tranches as well as second-lien loans, both of which are less-liquid.

Industry: From a sector standpoint, we are overweight Basic Industry across largely Building, Construction & (specialty) Chemical issuers, which exhibit lower cyclicality. We find value in Energy, particularly mid and downstream issuers which tend to be more resilient during periods of volatile commodity prices. Lastly, we are overweight Packaging driven by the sector’s exposure to resilient end markets, preferring “agnostic” suppliers with diverse and stable end markets.

On the other hand, our key underweights include Non-Cellular Telecom, Healthcare (DOGE exposure), and Consumer (DOGE and tariff exposure). We are underweight Healthcare as rising labor costs are pinching margins; the sector also has a higher proportion of smaller-sized credits which we find unappealing from a technical standpoint. Lastly, Consumer Services is largely comprised of smaller, sponsor-backed issuers; our underweight to the sector reflects our preference for larger, more liquid borrowers with better industry positioning.

Risk Considerations

data.title: CommScope: Better Days To Come Post CCS Divestiture
data.description:
Investment Thesis

When I first wrote about CommScope Holding Company (NASDAQ:COMM), I was beating the drum as a Strong Buy Pick. I was confident that the hidden value in its core business would roar once the company shed its burdens. And fast forward to today, I would say that "the deal has materialized." CommScope is now set to emerge as a leaner, virtually debt-free RemainCo, which will now be focused on broadband access and enterprise WiFi. Since my initial coverage, the stock has increased more than 100%. One of the major reasons why the stock jumped significantly, is due to the transformative sale announcement and hence reflecting much of that good news.

As a result, I am maintaining my Strong Buy rating on COMM. I believe that COMM's fundamentals have improved drastically, with 2Q FY2025 seeing a 32% increase y/y in net sales and a 79% y/y increase in adjusted EBITDA. I would also like to mention that management raised its EBITDA guidance by 15%. Now, the company expects between $1.15 billion and $1.20 billion, as noted by the CEO:

"...RemainCo is well positioned to deliver year- over-year growth in 2025 with strong cash flow generation. As we move through the second half of the year, we will provide updates on the pending transaction and positioning of RemainCo as appropriate..."

Charles L. Treadway, CEO, 2Q FY2025 Earnings Results

The Deal That Changed Everything

In August 2025, COMM announced a definitive agreement to sell its largest division, the Connectivity and Cable Solutions Segment [CCS] to Amphenol (APH). The agreement was for a $10.5 billion ticket size, in cash.

Now, one would wonder, why would COMM sell its largest business segment?

Well, this divestiture isn't just a financial windfall but rather a strategic about-face for the company. The CCS segment, which comprised fiber, copper cabling, and telecom infrastructure products, was expected to contribute $3.6 billion in sales in 2025, as per the earnings transcript.

Divesting from this segment is a strategic move. It's like an elephant shedding extra weight. And this is because it fundamentally changes COMM's shape and agility. As a result of the divestiture, COMM will now operate a much leaner structure called RemainCo (this consists of the ANS and Ruckus). This ideally means that after the deal, CommScope becomes a pure play on broadband access and enterprise wireless networking.

So, let's dive into why the deal is great and why it will change a lot for COMM.

First, the estimated proceeds from the deal are estimated at $10 billion in net proceeds, and this allows CommScope to;

"...repay all of our debt, redeeming our preferred equity and adding modest leverage on the remaining company, we would expect to have significant excess cash. We expect to distribute this excess cash to our shareholders as a dividend within 60 to 90 days following the closing of the proposed transaction after taking into account all relevant factors..."

Charles L. Treadway, CEO, 2Q FY2025 Earnings Results

In fact, over 70% of the net proceeds will go into paying all the debt owed, and another portion will be returned to shareholders within 2-3 months as observed.

"...I'm excited to announce this transformational deal that unlocks equity value, returns cash to our shareholders and strengthens the business. Our equity price was not reflective of the true value of our company. This transaction now brings improved clarity to CommScope equity value..."

Charles L. Treadway, CEO, 2Q FY2025 Earnings Results

I note that after the deal, COMM's balance sheet goes from a highly geared company to net cash, and the market confidence will have been partly restored.

The Recovery Play

The remaining two segments have staged an impressive recovery in 2025. This is after being in ashes in 2024. In 2Q 2025, ANS and RUCKUS combined delivered $513 million in revenue, which is a 58% increase from last year, and $127 million in adjusted EBITDA, a 326% y/y increase. This is one of the best numbers the company has had in quite a long time. And I believe, with only two core businesses, COMM is back in growth mode with improving profitability.

I also observe that COMM scores a B Growth Grade. The company has an A+ in terms of FWD EBIT growth and operating cash flow growth, as shown below. COMM also expects to increase its cash balances by about $125 million in 2025, which translates to over $200 million of capex and working capital. And this is a sign that the core business is now self-funding. However, I note that until the deal closes, net leverage remains as high as 6.6x EBITDA. With the current debt load of $9 billion, it means that by the next 1-year that leverage will vanish.

Valuation

After the stock's post-announcement surge, I still believe COMM is still undervalued relative to peers. Since my last article, COMM is no longer dirt cheap, but still looks discounted against the sector median.

First of all, its FWD non-GAAP P/E stands at 12.65x, while the sector median is at 24.07x, suggesting a 47.45% discount relative to the sector. Additionally, its FWD EV/EBITDA shows that COMM is trading at 9.88x while the sector median stands at 14.83x. Now, when I look at some of the peers in the networking hardware space, I note that the majority of them often command a double-digit EV/EBITDA, which is similar to the sector median. For instance, Cisco (CSCO) trades at around ~12x EV/EBITDA, while Corning (GLW) trades at roughly 18x and Fortinet (FTNT) trades at 24x.

I note that now that COMM is on the verge of becoming a net cash company, if I adjust for the upcoming debt paydown, then its pro forma P/E would likely drop significantly. (With the assumption that RemainCo profitability holds up). With the sector median trading at ~24x, a lower P/E for COMM would significantly imply a lower number than the current 12x P/E. And this will eventually suggest a significant discount to the sector median.

CommScope's Key Risk

The biggest risk I see for COMM at the moment is cyclical demand and customer concentration. Now that COMM has two major business segments, ANS segment is project-based and cyclical in nature. In fact, management has admitted that ANS has a higher customer concentration than Ruckus. This means that if one or two major customers delay upgrades or choose their rival for a new solution, ANS could see a sharp drop in orders.

Final Thoughts

In summary, I believe the company's management made the right call by divesting from CCS. And the fruits of that action are yet to materialize. I believe the company will reap big given that RemainCo is expected to revitalize growth and fix COMM's balance sheet. And for these reasons, I maintain my Strong Buy position.

data.title: Recession, Inflation, Or Goldilocks - What's Your Bet?
data.description:
Current Situation: Stagflation

We are currently in a stagflationary environment. The labor market is weakening, and real growth is slowing, while inflation is rising.

Based on the Fed's view, the labor market is weakening primarily due to the immigration policy. Specifically, due to the strict immigration policy, mass deportations, and voluntary departures, the labor supply is decreasing. At the same time, the demand for labor is also decreasing. Thus, the job creation is much weaker, and yet the unemployment rate remains low. Powell views this as a "low-hire low-fire market." The risk in this situation is that if the layoffs increase, the unemployment rate would likely spike quickly – and cause a recession.

Based on the Fed's view, inflation is rising primarily due to tariffs via rising prices of goods. However, this rise in inflation is likely to be only temporary, as companies gradually adjust their prices to reflect the higher cost of imports.

Thus, the Fed decided to focus more on a weakening labor market and to ignore inflation. Accordingly, it lowered the Federal fund rate by 25 bps at the September meeting and signaled two more cuts in 2025 and one additional cut in 2026 to the 3.4% level.

The financial markets are currently positioned:

  • 2-year yield (US2Y) is positioned for more aggressive cutting to 2.8% by the end of 2026.
  • 10Y yield (US10Y) has been falling, driven by the lower real yield, which signals low growth expectations, but also with steady and elevated breakeven inflation expectations at just below the critical 2.5% level, which signals that inflation remains a worry. Together, that reflects stagflation.
  • The S&P 500 (SP500) has been in a melt-up mode, supported by the idea that the Fed cuts will support the bubble, similarly to 1998.
  • Gold (GLD) continues to rise, reflecting all the risks ignored by other assets, including geopolitics.

So, what happens next?

The future path for financial assets will depend on how the current stagflationary environment evolves. There are three possible scenarios: 1) Recession, 2) Inflation, or 3) Goldilocks.

The Recession Scenario

In the recessionary scenario, the labor market continues to weaken and the unemployment rate spikes. The Fed is unable to prevent the recession with the cuts because the reasons behind the spike in the unemployment rate are not cyclical – rather they are structural or policy-based.

The Fed cuts cannot cause the increase in the labor force by bringing the immigrants back into the country or by reversing the aging of retiring baby boomers.

In this scenario, the financial markets would react as follows:

  • The Fed would be forced to cut more aggressively, which means 2Y yields would fall. However, 2Y yields are already pricing an aggressive Fed easing, which suggests that 2Y yields are already pricing a recession.
  • 10Y yields would fall toward 3% as real rates fall, and assuming that a recession causes inflation to fall, the breakeven inflation expectations would fall as well.
  • The stock market bubble would burst as earnings estimates fall and the PE valuations contract, with the sell-off similar to 2000 and 2008.

The Inflation Scenario

In the inflationary scenario, inflation becomes more persistent and potentially causes the Fed to pause the easing cycle and start increasing interest rates.

The labor market would get stronger, possibly due to the foreign direct investment (FDI) as committed to the Trump administration. There would be an increase in demand for construction workers to build factories and also for workers to work in those factories. Further, due to the labor shortage, the wages would rise, and this would trigger the price-wage inflationary spiral.

In this scenario, the financial markets would react as follows.

  • 2Y yields would rise as the Fed would be forced to get hawkish.
  • 10Y yields would rise above 5% as growth accelerates and inflation expectations increase towards the 3% level.
  • The stock market would likely have a deep correction like in 2022.
  • Gold would likely also correct as real yields increase.

The Goldilocks

The Goldilocks scenario would happen if the labor market gets stronger, growth accelerates, but inflation falls.

The labor market would get stronger, as explained in the inflationary scenario. However, in the Goldilocks case, inflation falls, as wages don't increase. This scenario could be possible if the AI boosts productivity with automatization in factories and with agentic AI for services.

In this case,

  • The Fed would be able to normalize interest rates, according to the current plans, which would push 2Y yields slightly higher.
  • The 10Y yield would stabilize at around 3.5-4%.
  • The stock market bubble could continue to inflate, although the ride could be volatile, interrupted by 10% corrections.

My Take

The current stagflation will have to evolve; either we will have a recession, higher inflation, or Goldilocks.

The market is pricing all three scenarios: 2Y yields are pricing an aggressive easing consistent with a recession, the 10Y breakeven inflation is suggesting inflation is still a worry, while the stock market is fully pricing the Goldilocks.

The key variable here is whether the foreign countries and companies will really invest in the US next year as committed. In addition, whether the expectations that the AI will boost productivity and lower inflation prove to be correct.

At this point I am skeptical about the Goldilocks. For example, the EU already suggested that it can't guarantee the $600 billion investment in the US. In my opinion, many other countries will possibly delay the committed investment and wait for the mid-terms, and the Supreme Court decision on the legality of tariffs could also be a game-changer. I am also skeptical about the AI, given the MIT study that finds that 95% of AI projects fail.

In that sense, I also view the inflationary scenario as less likely – the wages are unlikely to increase as the labor market is unlikely to bounce.

Thus, I view the recession scenario as most likely. The most important variable here is the fact that the Fed is fighting the policy events with lower interest rates – and that's likely to be ineffective. The monetary policy is effective only with cyclical events.

As a result, I see the stock market bubble burst as very likely.

Note, I do understand the Goldilocks scenario for all the bear critics, but at this point, it's all hype and hope. But we will know; let's see if the construction and manufacturing jobs pick up while the wage growth stays muted.
